chunks.title: Introduction to Airport Health Concerns
chunks.summary: The conversation begins with a mention of the sedentary nature of airport travel and the health repercussions of inactivity, highlighting the statistics related to American obesity and metabolic dysfunction. Paul Saladino emphasizes the need for more active solutions in airports to address these issues.
chunks.transcript: I started a conversation. My daughter pointed out a video from uh Dr. Paul Saladino where he was complaining about there was no pull-up bars in airports uh which prompted me to call Secretary Kennedy to give me Paul's number to start the conversation. But uh he's been a leader of in and thinking about what do we have for food choices but also movement choices in airports. And so I'm grateful that he's here today with us. Uh Paul Saladino. >> Thank you Secretary Duffy. It's good to be here with you both. When you think about it, airports are one of the most sedentary places that we are as Americans. The traveling experience in general is probably the single most sedentary thing that we do. Between sitting in an Uber to the airport, sitting in the airport, waiting for your flight, delayed flight, sitting on a flight for 3 or 4 hours, getting to your destination, sitting in another Uber, it's a very sedentary thing to travel. And as Secretary Kennedy says, 3 million Americans every day are traveling and doing this and engaging in sedentary activities or lack of activities. What we know very clearly is that when we are sedentary, this is bad for us as humans. Even one single hour of sitting in inactivity can create vascular dysfunction within our bodies. Two to three hours of sitting not moving at all can increase inflammatory markers in the human body. We know that less than half of all Americans get a recommended amount of activity. And that 70 plus% of Americans are obese and overweight. Between being overweight, metabolic dysfunction, and the level to which we are, sedentary and inactive as Americans, these are directly linked to the things that kill us most as Americans, to cardiovascular disease, to diabetes, to obesity. And yet, a lot of this could be made better. As someone that travels a lot for my own work, I know this firsthand. I'm in airports traveling to do podcasts or make videos to share information with people across the country and it's hard for me to be a weird guy in the corner doing air squats or doing a handstand. Nobody I mean it's not really encouraged in airports to do that kind of stuff. So I love Secretary Duffy's intention to make airports more understanding. But I also am super excited about the possibility of getting mini gyms, little airport workout spaces for humans. We know according to a 2025 study that over $190 billion dollars of health care spend is directly attributable to inactivity in Americans. That's a staggering number. What if we we could create small spaces for humans in airports for Americans in airports to get many workouts? You don't have to get super sweaty, but what if we could put pull-up bars and dip bars and parallettes for push-ups or for lsits? If we could put boxes for step-ups. Maybe put some treadmills for people who want to walk near their gate. We can put some bars for stretching. We can put yoga mats and maybe some rolling pins for people to move their muscles. Anything to get people moving. Maybe some exercise bikes. These would be a massive step in the right direction. And it's such a huge thing that we could do for Americans. Whenever I talk to people about this, they're super excited. Anyone I've talked to about the idea has just been they smile. They get excited. Oh, a mini gym in an airport. That's great. I would use that for sure. I think if we could pair this with healthier food options, unprocessed foods, unprocessed meat, unprocessed plant foods, we could really help all of these millions of Americans every day that are traveling. So, I want to commend Secretary Duffy and Secretary Kennedy for spearheading this effort. I mean, a billion dollars of grants to help El airports improve the quality of health for travelers every day, including mini gyms for all of us to move. This is incredible. We've got a pull-up bar set up behind us here. So, I'm definitely going to get some pull-ups in with Secretary Duffy and Secretary Kennedy after this. But that's what that's there to signify, guys. The beginning of movement spaces for humans and airports. Hopefully, we'll start with DCA and Dulles. But I'm excited to be a part of this. I'm grateful to be a part of this. I'm humbled that a piece of content that I did could have been seen by Secretary Duffy and that we could be a part of bringing this this this actual valuable resource to Americans throughout the country. Now, so I'm super grateful to be a part of implementing this and amplifying this message. And again, I want to commend Secretary Duffy and Secretary Kennedy for spearheading this effort. I think this is amazing. Modern life is not set up for us to be healthy as humans, but let's keep making small steps in that direction so that we can all become healthier and then that directly leads to us being happier, more gracious, and more caring with everyone around us. So, I'm super excited to be a part of this effort. Thank you both for this

chunks.title: The Importance of Movement in Airports
chunks.summary: Saladino discusses the detrimental effects of prolonged inactivity on health, linking it to conditions like cardiovascular disease and obesity. He proposes the idea of mini gyms in airports to promote physical activity among travelers.

chunks.title: Innovative Solutions for Healthier Travel
chunks.summary: The segment outlines potential features of mini gyms, including pull-up bars and yoga mats, and highlights the excitement surrounding healthier food options at airports. Saladino commends the efforts by airport authorities to improve health for travelers.

chunks.title: Conclusion and Call to Action
chunks.summary: Saladino wraps up by expressing gratitude for the opportunity to discuss these initiatives and remains hopeful for future improvements in airport health facilities. He emphasizes the connection between physical health and overall well-being.
chunks.transcript: to us being happier, more gracious, and more caring with everyone around us. So, I'm super excited to be a part of this effort. Thank you both for this opportunity. >> Well done.
